items.text: We did do some things together, perhaps went to the cinema and so on, but certainly, it was a surprise to me when he said... 'Would you consider marrying me?'
items.context: Recalling Alan Turing's marriage proposal to her in 1941 at Bletchley Park
items.publicReaction: The 1992 BBC interview was widely cited in later biographical and historical accounts; generated renewed public interest in Clarke's personal relationship with Turing
items.source: BBC Horizon documentary episode 'The Strange Life and Death of Dr. Turing'

items.text: Now the next day, I suppose we went for a bit of a walk together, after lunch. He told me that he had this homosexual tendency.
items.context: Describing the day after Turing's marriage proposal when he disclosed his homosexuality to her
items.publicReaction: This statement became key evidence in discussions about Turing's sexuality and his personal struggles; cited in multiple biographies
items.source: BBC Horizon documentary episode 'The Strange Life and Death of Dr. Turing'

items.text: Naturally, that worried me a bit, because I did know that was something which was almost certainly permanent, but we carried on.
items.context: Response to learning about Turing's homosexuality and their decision to continue their engagement despite this revelation
items.publicReaction: Widely quoted in biographies and articles about both Clarke and Turing; demonstrates her pragmatic and progressive attitude for the 1940s
items.source: BBC Horizon documentary episode 'The Strange Life and Death of Dr. Turing'; also cited in Wikipedia and IBTimes articles

items.text: His mother wrote to me, and she said that although it was a verdict of suicide, she believed it an accident, and of course, his method was chosen to make it possible for some at least to believe that.
items.context: Discussing Alan Turing's death in 1954 and his mother's letter to Clarke about the circumstances
items.publicReaction: This quote was cited in Historical vs. Hollywood and other sources examining the true nature of Turing's death
items.source: BBC Horizon documentary episode 'The Strange Life and Death of Dr. Turing'

items.context: Clarke's humorous response written on official forms, after being promoted to the grade of Linguist at Bletchley Park to increase her pay, despite not speaking additional languages
items.publicReaction: This anecdote became widely cited in biographical accounts as an illustration of her wit and the absurdity of wartime bureaucracy at Bletchley Park
items.source: Multiple biographical sources including MacTutor History of Mathematics Archive and Spartacus Educational

items.text: I can remember Alan Turing coming in as usual for a day's leave, doing his own mathematical research at night, in the warmth and light of the office, without interrupting the routine of daytime sleep.
items.context: Recollection of Alan Turing's working habits and dedication at Hut 8 during World War II
items.publicReaction: Cited in biographical accounts as evidence of Turing's exceptional work ethic and commitment to the cryptanalytic effort
items.date: no specific date given; retrospective recollection
items.source: Spartacus Educational biography of Joan Clarke

items.text: It would have been too painful.
items.context: Clarke's reason for declining to watch or attend performances of Hugh Whitmore's 1987 play 'Breaking the Code,' which featured a character based on her and dramatized her relationship with Alan Turing
items.publicReaction: This statement was cited in multiple sources as evidence of how deeply the personal relationship with Turing affected her, even decades after the war and their broken engagement
items.source: MacTutor History of Mathematics Archive and biographical sources

items.context: Clarke's written chapter contribution to the edited volume 'Codebreakers: The Inside Story of Bletchley Park,' discussing her work on the naval Enigma cipher
items.publicReaction: Published contribution was well-received by historians and cryptography scholars as a first-hand account from one of the key personnel involved in breaking the naval Enigma
items.source: Codebreakers: The Inside Story of Bletchley Park, edited by F.H. Hinsley and Alan Stripp, Oxford University Press, pages 113-118

items.text: Appendix 30: The Polish, French and British contributions to the solution of the Enigma
items.context: Clarke co-authored an appendix with Sir Harry Hinsley correcting the historical record about the respective contributions of British, French, and Polish cryptanalysts to breaking the Enigma, addressing inaccuracies in earlier accounts
items.publicReaction: This scholarly contribution was important for historical accuracy and recognized Clarke's role in reassessing the Polish contribution to cryptanalysis during the war
items.source: British Intelligence in the Second World War, Volume 3, Part 2, revised edition
